trajectory_summary: The call opened with genuine customer engagement — Kevin was curious, vetting actively, and volunteering values. Holland established basic legitimacy and moved through verify competently, but never converted Kevin's stated values (sustainability, reliability, ability to pay) into emotional alignment. The call ended on a text-loop deferral with no locked next step, leaving the lead structurally fragile. Kevin's parting engagement (asking where Holland is located, chatting about Utah weather) suggests his interest hasn't fully cooled — the lead is not dead, but it is unanchored.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the call did not produce a booked appointment — it ended with Holland promising to text Kevin and Kevin agreeing to 'get back to you guys.' This is a soft callback with no locked time, which the schema distinguishes sharply from a booked sit. If the v1 system tagged this as 'booked' or 'appointment set,' that tag should be disputed. The correct classification is 'open text loop / unanchored callback.'

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Converting a situational deferral into a locked next step
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Okay. Yeah. I'll send a message to you, just right after this call, and then we can kinda finish up through text.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland accepted Kevin's 'text me' exit without attempting to lock a callback time — the single move that separates an open text loop from a scheduled sit. Kevin was not hostile; he was distracted by a grandchild. The window to lock a time was open and Holland didn't take it. This is the call's most actionable gap because it's a one-question fix ('When's a good time tomorrow?') that directly protects the setter's paycheck on a lead that showed genuine engagement.

key_moments.quote: So we're actually a a huge company. We are nationwide, and so we're we're we're all over. But, yeah, we have our Where is headquarters? Chicago.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland appeared to hesitate mid-sentence when Kevin asked about headquarters — the transcript shows 'we're we're we're all over' followed by what reads as a partial or uncertain answer before Kevin's follow-up question prompted the correction to Utah. Any visible uncertainty on a basic company fact registers with a skeptical buyer who is actively vetting the company. Holland recovered quickly, but the stumble came on the first of Kevin's three legitimacy probes.

key_moments.quote: I do want to clarify. Is your roof it's just a regular asphalt shingle roof, it looks like. Yes. Do you do you know how old that is?
key_moments.stewart_read: Kevin's roof is 15-20 years old — right at the schema's hard-DQ threshold (roof_age_over_15_no_replacement_planned). Holland captured the data and responded 'Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Sounds good.' with no follow-up on replacement plans and no bridge to Ion's in-house roofing offering. This is a dual miss: a potential DQ risk that wasn't resolved, and a cross-sell signal that wasn't routed.

key_moments.quote: Well, not all not only the best deal, but there's a sustainability deal. You know what I mean? And reliability. You know what I mean? Ability. Yep. Right. Reliability and then, k. And most importantly, the ability to pay.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kevin volunteered a three-part values hierarchy — sustainability, reliability, ability to pay — unprompted. This is the richest emotional anchor of the call. The schema identifies verify.interest_in_solar as the moment where 'depth matters more than the answer' and where top setters spend 60+ seconds. Holland responded to each value with a single-word acknowledgment ('Yeah,' 'Yep,' 'Yeah. K.') and moved on. The bill-swap framing introduced earlier at 03:02 would have landed significantly harder if it had been tied back to Kevin's 'ability to pay' framing here.

key_moments.quote: So I will be honest with you. I'm not exactly sure, like, what the cost of your system would be, but I can say that we have a we don't do any money upfront or out of pocket.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's bill-swap pivot is directionally correct — she redirected a pricing question to the bill-replacement concept rather than fabricating a number. The honesty ('I'm not exactly sure') is appropriate. However, the pivot floated without grounding because Kevin's bill had not yet been established. The bill-swap framing ('replace your current bill with a cheaper solar bill') has no emotional weight when the listener's actual bill pain is unknown. This is a sequencing gap, not a protocol violation.
